compiled_state.author: Joe Oakhart

comments.author: Joe Oakhart
comments.body: AC0 proven LIVE against the real origin GitHub remote (github.com:navapbc/rebar) on 2026-07-04: a blob-pointing refs/reconciler/* ref pushed, was advertised by git ls-remote at exactly the blob OID, fetched back with payload byte-for-byte intact, and deleted. GitHub accepts blob-refs under refs/reconciler/* → ref->blob primary path used, ref->commit fallback not needed. Artifacts: scripts/prove_ref_lock_ac0.sh (repeatable proving command, run live) + tests/external/test_ref_lock_ac0_live.py (external-tier, passed against origin under REBAR_RUN_EXTERNAL=1) + hermetic bare-remote test. ADR 0031 updated with the proof.

compiled_state.description:
## What

Introduce the low-level **bare-ref CAS lock primitive** the reconciler pass-lock and phase-gate
will sit on. Ref -> blob, so it never lives in the tickets working tree and is never union-merged.
No reconciler wiring yet (C3). Also **prove the remote feasibility** the whole design rests on.

- **AC0 feasibility spike (do this first — it gates the rest):** prove a non-branch
  `refs/reconciler/*` ref pointing at a **blob** round-trips (push + fetch) through the `origin`
  GitHub remote using the CI `GITHUB_TOKEN` (contents:write). If GitHub rejects it, fall back to
  ref -> tiny-commit and record that in the ADR (fallback fully specified under Design decisions).
  Document the working refspec.
- New module `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_ref_lock.py` exposing `acquire`/`release`/`read`
  over a configurable ref name (`refs/reconciler/lock`, `refs/reconciler/gate` share one schema).
- **Blob = newline-terminated UTF-8 JSON** `{holder, lease_secs, heartbeat_ns, fence}`, clock from
  `time.time_ns()` (NOT `rebar._store.hlc` — that fcntl cache orders ticket events, wrong tool).
  `heartbeat_ns` is the acquisition/renew anchor; `fence` is a monotonic **progress-witness /
  generation counter** (seeded to 0 on first acquire; C2 increments it on renew/steal) — not a full
  fencing token. Decode of a corrupt/partial/empty blob **fails closed**: `read` raises a named
  `RefLockCorruptError` and callers treat "cannot parse" as HELD, never free.
- **Blob creation:** `git hash-object -w --stdin` plants the blob and yields the OID, THEN
  `git update-ref <ref> <oid> <old>`: **create-only CAS acquire** uses old = 40 zeros; **release
  deletes the ref** (`git update-ref -d <ref> <old-oid>`) with old = the exact observed OID. A
  release whose CAS sees a different oid (someone else now owns it) is a benign **idempotent success**,
  not an error.
- **Reuse the real CAS helper** `_cas_advance_with_retry` (`_advisory_lock.py:447-487`), NOT a
  second primitive. Its discriminator `_is_cas_mismatch` (`_advisory_lock.py:415`, hard-coding
  `'refs/heads/tickets'` at line 425) is **generalized to accept the ref name** so a CAS exit-128 on
  `refs/reconciler/*` is classified correctly (exact seam under Design decisions). Reuse, do not
  duplicate the classifier.
- **Acquire vs release retry semantics differ** (so the reused retry loop must be told which path it
  is on): a create-only **acquire** CAS exit-128 means "lock already held" — definitive, **not
  retried**. A **release** CAS exit-128 (ref already gone / owned by another) is a benign **idempotent
  success**, not retried. **Steal**-CAS (C2) exit-128 is the single-winner race — the loser stops and
  does not re-acquire. Only genuinely transient CAS races are retried via `_cas_advance_with_retry`.
- No lease-expiry/steal logic here (that is C2); a plain `acquire` fails if the ref already exists.
- Add structured log emission for acquire-failure (already held) and release on a stale oid.
- Record the bare-ref-lock design decision (schema, CAS contract, remote refspec, operator runbook)
  in a new ADR under `docs/adr/` (extended by later children).

**Why a ref lock at all (vs the workflow's `concurrency:` group in `reconcile-bridge.yml`)?**
GitHub Actions `concurrency:` only serializes runs *within one repo's workflow*; a manually-invoked
pass, a cron on a different runner, or a second clone bypasses it entirely. The ref lock is a
cross-invocation, workflow-independent guard anchored on the shared remote — the workflow group and
the ref lock are complementary, not redundant.

## Design decisions (resolves plan-review ambiguities; binding on the implementation)

1. **`_is_cas_mismatch` seam (backward-compatible).** Add an optional `ref_name: str =
   "refs/heads/tickets"` parameter; the default preserves every existing call site verbatim. Thread
   the ref name through the one internal use (`_advisory_lock.py:469`) and update the one external
   call site (`_advisory_lock.py:585`) to pass `ref_name="refs/heads/tickets"` explicitly. No new
   shared module: `_ref_lock.py` imports `_cas_advance_with_retry` / `_is_cas_mismatch` from its
   sibling `_advisory_lock.py` (two call sites is below rule-of-three; C4 leaves these helpers in
   place). The coupling is documented in the ADR.
2. **Three distinct exit-128 outcomes without duplicating the classifier.** Factor a
   `_cas_once(...) -> bool` helper (True = CAS succeeded; False = CAS old-oid mismatch; re-raises any
   non-mismatch `CalledProcessError`). `_cas_advance_with_retry` becomes `_cas_once` in the retry
   loop (behaviour unchanged). `acquire` maps False -> raise `RefLockHeldError` (definitive, no
   retry); `release` maps False -> return (idempotent success); C2's steal maps False -> "lost the
   race", stop.
3. **`RefLockCorruptError(ValueError)`** is defined in `_ref_lock.py` and raised by `read` on any of:
   empty / non-UTF-8 bytes; invalid JSON; a missing `holder`/`lease_secs`/`heartbeat_ns`/`fence`
   field; `fence` not a non-negative int; `lease_secs` not a positive number. It carries the raw
   bytes for debugging. Callers treat it (and any read failure) as HELD, never free.
4. **`fence` in C1.** `acquire` writes `fence=0` unconditionally (create-only CAS guarantees no prior
   blob survived); `release` neither reads nor mutates `fence` (it deletes the ref). All `fence`
   increment / renew / steal logic is C2.
5. **Distributed CAS via an optional `remote`.** `acquire`/`release`/`read` take an optional
   `remote` argument. When `remote is None` (unit tests / local) they use a pure local
   `git update-ref` CAS. When a remote is given they use the network-CAS equivalent —
   `git fetch <remote> <ref>:<ref>` to sync, then `git push --force-with-lease=<ref>:<old-oid>`
   (explicit lease-ref form, never bare) — which is exactly the old-oid CAS on the authoritative
   remote. The reconciler passes `remote="origin"`; wiring that call and the workflow fetch refspec
   are C3. AC0 proves the remote path's refspec.
6. **Subprocess timeouts.** Every `subprocess.run` in `_ref_lock.py` sets `timeout=` — 5 s for local
   object/ref ops, 30 s for remote push/fetch — and a timeout raises `RefLockTimeoutError` (a
   fail-closed condition; callers treat it as "cannot determine state" = HELD). Values recorded in
   the ADR.
7. **AC0 ref->tiny-commit fallback (fully specified).** If GitHub rejects a blob-pointing ref, the
   ref points instead at a tiny commit whose tree holds a single `lock.json` with the same schema;
   `read` retrieves it via `git cat-file blob <ref>:lock.json` (commit case) vs `git cat-file blob
   <ref>` (blob case), selected by `git cat-file -t <ref>`; `git hash-object -w` still plants the
   payload. The CAS (create-only / observed-oid) is identical. The ADR records which path GitHub
   accepted.
8. **Operator break-glass.** The ADR carries a runbook: clear a wedged lock with
   `git push origin :refs/reconciler/lock` (remote) or `git update-ref -d refs/reconciler/lock`
   (local). C2's lease expiry makes this rarely necessary.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] AC0: a proving command/test demonstrates `refs/reconciler/*` (ref -> blob) push+fetch through
      the `origin` GitHub remote with the CI token; the working refspec (and the fully-specified
      ref->commit fallback) is documented in the ADR.
- [ ] `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_ref_lock.py` provides `acquire`/`release`/`read` over a
      configurable ref and an optional `remote`; acquire is create-only CAS (old=zeros), release is
      observed-oid CAS; blob is created via `git hash-object -w` before `update-ref` / push.
- [ ] Blob is UTF-8 JSON `{holder, lease_secs, heartbeat_ns, fence}` (`time.time_ns()`, `fence=0` on
      acquire); `read` of a corrupt/empty blob raises `RefLockCorruptError` and callers treat it as
      HELD (fail-closed), never free; the corrupt conditions in Design decision 3 are each covered.
- [ ] The CAS reuses `_cas_advance_with_retry` / `_is_cas_mismatch` (`_advisory_lock.py:447-487,415`)
      via the `_cas_once` seam; `_is_cas_mismatch` gains a `ref_name` param defaulting to
      `refs/heads/tickets` (existing call sites unchanged); acquire exit-128 ("held") is NOT retried,
      release exit-128 is idempotent success, and C2's steal exit-128 is single-winner-loss.
- [ ] Subprocess calls in `_ref_lock.py` carry timeouts (5 s local / 30 s remote) and a timeout
      raises `RefLockTimeoutError` (treated as HELD).
- [ ] Structured logs are emitted for acquire-already-held and stale-oid release.
- [ ] Unit tests cover: acquire on a free ref; second concurrent acquire fails (already exists);
      `read` on present vs absent ref (defined contract); release requires the correct old oid and a
      stale-oid release is idempotent success; corrupt-blob decode fails closed; and a parametrized
      regression that `_is_cas_mismatch` still classifies exit-128 on `refs/heads/tickets` unchanged
      AND classifies exit-128 on `refs/reconciler/lock`.
- [ ] The bare-ref-lock decision (schema, CAS contract, remote refspec, fallback, timeouts, operator
      runbook) is captured in a new `docs/adr/` entry. Non-integration suite green.

file_impact.reason: new bare-ref CAS lock primitive + blob schema

compiled_state.title: Ref-lock primitive: bare-ref CAS acquire/release + lock-blob schema
